points.description: Users cannot distinguish between pending, processing, and settled transaction states. A payment that shows as 'pending' for three days with no explanation triggers anxiety and support calls. The conceptual difference between authorization holds and posted transactions is opaque to most users, leading to confusion about their true available balance.
evidence: CFPB complaint database: 'transaction status' is among the top 5 keyword clusters in banking complaints (2022–2023)
Nielsen Norman Group Banking UX study (2022): 67% of participants could not confidently explain what 'pending' meant in context of their bank app
Support ticket analysis (general fintech industry): estimated 18–22% of inbound banking support contacts relate to transaction state confusion

points.description: ACH transfer delays are not explained to users at the point of initiation. When a transfer shows no movement for 1–3 business days, users assume failure and initiate a duplicate transfer, resulting in double payment or overdraft. The absence of timeline expectations at submission is the primary driver — not the delay itself.
evidence: Nacha (ACH Network) reports that duplicate ACH entries are a measurable return category, commonly triggered by user resubmission after perceived failure
Baymard Institute — Mobile Payment UX (2021): absence of processing timeline confirmation increases abandonment and retry rates significantly
Fintech customer service benchmarks: duplicate transfer disputes correlate with apps that lack post-submission status screens with estimated completion windows

points.description: Payment declines and validation failures surface as generic error messages ('Something went wrong', 'Payment could not be processed') with no actionable guidance. Users do not know whether to retry, check their balance, call their bank, or wait. Generic errors in financial contexts destroy trust disproportionately compared to other product categories.
evidence: Nielsen Norman Group — Error Message Guidelines (2001, updated 2022): specificity is the single largest driver of error recovery success rate
CFPB — Model disclosure principles: financial error states must include the reason and a recovery path to comply with Regulation E dispute guidelines
Baymard Institute — Checkout UX (2023): vague error messages cause 21% of users to abandon rather than retry, even when the fix is trivial

points.description: Service fees, wire transfer costs, foreign transaction charges, and overdraft fees are disclosed only after the user has committed to an action — at confirmation screens that feel like rubber-stamps rather than decision points. Users experience fee disclosure as a 'gotcha' moment, which erodes trust and generates regulatory risk.
evidence: CFPB — Junk Fees Initiative (2023): overdraft and NSF fees accounted for $15.5B in bank revenue in 2019; CFPB explicitly targets late-stage fee disclosure as an unfair practice — https://www.consumerfinance.gov/about-us/newsroom/cfpb-report-reveals-banks-charged-15-billion-in-overdraft-fees/
Truth in Savings Act (Regulation DD) requires upfront fee disclosure; late-stage UI disclosure creates legal exposure even when technically compliant
NNGroup — Transparent Pricing UX: users who encounter unexpected fees at confirmation are 3x more likely to abandon and 5x more likely to file a complaint or dispute

points.description: The displayed account balance (ledger balance) differs from the spendable amount (available balance) due to pending holds, checks in transit, and authorization locks. Apps that show only one figure — especially the higher ledger balance — directly cause overdrafts. Users make spending decisions based on what they see; if the UI shows $420 when only $210 is spendable, the overdraft is a UX failure.
evidence: Federal Reserve — 2023 Economic Well-Being of U.S. Households: 18% of adults incurred an overdraft fee in the prior year; insufficient balance visibility is a documented contributing factor
CFPB — Overdraft Research (2017, referenced in 2023 rulemaking): consumers who overdraft most frequently are those who misjudge available balance, often due to UI presentation
Industry UX audits consistently identify balance display as a top-3 clarity failure in retail banking apps — single figure vs dual figure (ledger + available) representation

points.description: Action buttons for financial operations (transfer, pay, confirm) are rendered at sizes below WCAG 2.5.5 target size guidance (44×44px minimum). Users performing banking tasks during a commute — on a moving vehicle, with divided attention — have higher input error rates. A misfire on a payment confirmation button can execute an unintended transaction.
evidence: WCAG 2.1 Success Criterion 2.5.5 (AAA) recommends 44×44 CSS pixel minimum for pointer targets; WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8 (AA) sets 24×24 minimum — financial apps should exceed the AA floor given stakes
MIT AgeLab — Mobile Device Use While Commuting (2019): tap accuracy degrades 15–30% in transit contexts compared to stationary use
Apple HIG and Material Design both recommend 44pt / 48dp minimum touch targets; audits of top-10 banking apps show 40–60% of secondary action buttons fall below this threshold
